figures.caption: Fig. 11. Dirichlet distribution and comparison of KL divergence from the uniform distribution. The 1-class distribution has the largest KL divergence, indicating the most severe data heterogeneity.

Fig. 9. Convergence performance on Fashion-MNIST with Dirichlet distribution (𝛼 = 0.1). Each device has 1000 unlabeled shared samples, and the total number of training samples is 10000. The black and colored lines represent average and individual device accuracies, respectively. Although DecFedAvg achieves the highest average accuracy, the proposed FedF-ADMM attains the smallest accuracy gap among devices.

Fig. 10. Convergence performance on CIFAR-10 with Dirichlet distribution (𝛼 = 0.1). Each device has 5000 unlabeled shared samples, and the total number of training samples is 50000. The black and colored lines represent average and individual device accuracies, respectively. Similar to the FashionMNIST results, DecFedAvg achieves the highest average accuracy, while the proposed FedF-ADMM attains the smallest accuracy gap among devices.

Fig. 8. Tuning results on CIFAR-10 with the 1-class distribution. FedF-ADMM outperforms the other algorithms in terms of accuracy. In addition, the stabilization coefficient 𝜈 contributes to improving training stability against variations in the other hyperparameters.
TABLE IV COMPARISON OF AVERAGE ACCURACY AND ACCURACY GAP.
TABLE III CNN ARCHITECTURE USED IN THE EXPERIMENTS.

Fig. 2. System model of FedF-ADMM. Devices collaboratively train their local models on their own datasets by communicating with one another. They exchange distilled knowledge on a shared dataset to achieve consensus.

Fig. 14. Comparison of convergence performance across different network topologies. Black and colored lines represent average and individual device accuracies, respectively. The convergence speed and final accuracy remain comparable, although the star topology suppresses fluctuations.

Fig. 4. Convergence performance on Fashion-MNIST. Each device has 500 samples from 2 labels and 1000 unlabeled shared samples. The black line indicates the average accuracy across devices, while colored lines represent individual device accuracies. The proposed FedF-ADMM achieves higher accuracy, better consensus, and faster convergence than other algorithms.

Fig. 5. Convergence performance on Fashion-MNIST. Each device has 1000 samples from 1 label and 1000 unlabeled shared samples. The black and colored lines represent average and individual device accuracies, respectively. Because of the severe data heterogeneity, individual devices exhibit large fluctuations in accuracy in DecFedProx and DFedADMM. In contrast, the proposed FedF-ADMM shows stable convergence and outperforms other algorithms.

Fig. 6. Convergence performance on CIFAR-10. Each device has 2500 samples from 2 labels and 5000 unlabeled shared samples. The black and colored lines represent average and individual device accuracies, respectively. Compared with Fashion-MNIST results, when using parameter-level aggregation methods (DecFedAvg, DecFedProx, etc.), the accuracies of individual models fluctuate significantly. CMFD shows stable convergence, but the proposed FedF-ADMM achieves much higher accuracy and better consensus.

Fig. 7. Convergence performance on CIFAR-10. Each device has 5000 samples from 1 label and 5000 unlabeled shared samples. The black and colored lines represent average and individual device accuracies, respectively. In this severe non-IID setting, Parameter-level aggregation methods achieve only low accuracy. Even in such a case, the proposed FedF-ADMM does not suffer from significant performance degradation.
